sections.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD AND/ OR A NUMBER for each answer.
sections.passage_or_context:
Buckworth Conservation Group

Regular activities
Beach
• making sure the beach does not have (1) {{BLANK}} on it
• no (2) {{BLANK}}

Nature reserve
• maintaining paths
• nesting boxes for birds installed
• next task is taking action to attract (3) {{BLANK}} to the place

• building a new (5) {{BLANK}}

Forthcoming events Saturday
• meet at Dunsmore Beach car park
• walk across the sands and reach the (6) {{BLANK}}

Woodwork session

questions.content: Buckworth Conservation Group notes with blanks (1)-(10).

sections.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B or C for Questions 11-14. Choose TWO letters, A-E for Questions 15-20.

questions.content: What is the maximum number of people who can stand on each side of the boat?
options: A 9

questions.content: What color are the tour boats?
options: A dark red
B jet black
C light green

questions.content: Which lunchbox is suitable for someone who does not eat meat or fish?
options: A 1
B 2
C 3

questions.content: What should people do with their litter?
options: A take it home
B hand it to a member of staff
C put it in bins provided on the boat

questions.content: Which TWO features of the lighthouse does Lou mention?
options: A why it was built
B who built it
C how long it took to build
D who staffed it
E what it was built with

questions.content: Which TWO types of creature might come close to the boat?
options: A sea eagles
B fur seals
C dolphins
D whales
E penguins

questions.content: Which TWO points does Lou make about the caves?
options: A Only large tourist boats can visit them.
B The entrances to them are often blocked.
C It is too dangerous for individuals to go near them
D Someone will explain what is inside them.
E They cannot be reached on foot.

sections.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B or C for Questions 21-26. For Questions 27-30, choose FOUR answers from the box and write the correct letter, A-F, next to questions 27-30.
sections.passage_or_context: Work experience for veterinary science students

questions.content: What problem did both Diana and Tim have when arranging their work experience?
options: A making initial contact with suitable farms
B organising transport to and from the farm
C finding a placement for the required length of time

questions.content: Tim was pleased to be able to help
options: A a lamb that had a broken leg.
B a sheep that was having difficulty giving birth.
C a newly born lamb that was having trouble feeding.

questions.content: Diana says the sheep on her farm
options: A were of various different varieties.
B were mainly reared for their meat.
C had better quality wool than sheep on the hills.

questions.content: What did the students learn about adding supplements to chicken feed?
options: A These should only be given if specially needed.
B It is worth paying extra for tie most effective ones.
C The amount given at one time should be limited.

questions.content: What happened when Diana was working with dairy cows?
options: A She identified some cows incorrectly.
B She accidentally threw some milk away.
C She made a mistake when storing milk.

questions.content: What did both farmers mention about vets and farming?
options: A Vets are failing to cope with some aspects of animal health.
B There needs to be a fundamental change in the training of vets.
C Some jobs could be done by the farmer rather than by a vet

questions.content: What opinion do the students give about each of the following modules on their veterinary science course?
options: A Tim found this easier than expected.
B Tim thought this was not very clearly organised.
C Diana may do some further study on this.
D They both found the reading required for this was difficult.
E Tim was shocked at something he learned on this module.
F They were both surprised how little is known about some aspects of this.
27. Medical terminology
28. Diet and nutrition
29. Animal disease
30. Wildlife medication

sections.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.
sections.passage_or_context:
Labyrinths

Definition
• a winding spiral path leading to a central area

Labyrinths compared with mazes
• Mazes are a type of (31) {{BLANK}}
– (32) {{BLANK}} is needed to navigate through a maze
– the word ‘maze’ is derived from a word meaning a feeling of (33) {{BLANK}}
• Labyrinths represent a journey through life
– they have frequently been used in (34) {{BLANK}} and prayer

Early examples of the labyrinth spiral
• Ancient carvings on (35) {{BLANK}} have been found across many cultures
• The Pima, a Native American tribe, wove the symbol on baskets
• Ancient Greeks used the symbol on (36) {{BLANK}}

Walking labyrinths
• The largest surviving example of a turf labyrinth once had a big (37) {{BLANK}} at its centre

Labyrinths nowadays
• Believed to have a beneficial impact on mental and physical health, e.g., walking a maze can reduce a person’s (38) {{BLANK}} rate
• Used in medical and health and fitness settings and also prisons
• Popular with patients, visitors and staff in hospitals
– patients who can’t walk can use ‘finger labyrinths’ made from (39) {{BLANK}}
– research has shown that Alzheimer’s sufferers experience less (40) {{BLANK}}
